one_line_takeaway: In a 14-day, 303-bounty snapshot of the RentAHuman.ai marketplace, the authors show that 32.7% of postings come through MCP/API programmatic channels (with corroborating burst-timing, template-reuse, and callback-URL automation signatures) and, via a kappa=0.86/0.81 dual-coder process, identify six classes of abuse (credential fraud, identity proxy, reconnaissance, social-media manipulation, OTP solicitation, referral fraud) purchasable at a median $25/worker, while a simple seven-rule content-screening baseline they built retrospectively catches 51 of these cases with just one false positive out of 303.
key_finding: 32.7% of the 303 bounties (99) originated from programmatic channels (47 MCP via agent_ prefix, 10 distinct IDs; 52 REST API via apikey_ prefix, 18 distinct keys), a figure the authors treat as a lower bound since automated browser sessions would be misclassified as user_ (web). Programmatic-channel posts showed markedly higher burst rates than web posts (41.8% of programmatic pairs <60s apart vs. 16.7% for web; median inter-arrival 240s vs. 2,021s). The dual-coder process identified six abuse classes across security-relevant bounties: Credential & Account Fraud (n=8, e.g., mass Gmail/Google Voice creation at $12-15 for up to 100 spots), Identity Proxy (n=4, e.g., a $60/hr, 30-hour, $1,800-total bounty to impersonate someone in job interviews), Reconnaissance & Verification (n=12, including a GroundAssist automated phone-verification pipeline of 7 API bounties with Cloudflare-tunnel/localhost callback URLs, and GPS-tagged photography requests), Social Media Manipulation (n=5, e.g., 500-spot follow campaigns at $1/spot), OTP/2FA Solicitation (n=1, $60, 79 applications), and Referral & Promotion Fraud (n=5, crypto-exchange KYC referral abuse at $3-5). Median per-worker price across the abuse-relevant bounties was $25; credential fraud showed median exposure exceeding $1,000 (price x max spots). The retrospective seven-rule content-screening evaluation flagged 52/303 bounties (17.2%), with 51 true positives against dual-coder labels and only 1 false positive (R3, off-platform-contact indicators, had the broadest reach at 18 flags). The authors also report a structural 'engage-then-ghost' vulnerability: 35 of 37 'completed' bounties showed spotsFilled = 0, and their own single-bounty probe (following an Instagram page) was never accepted/paid, indicating the platform's escrow design lets requesters extract the desired action during the application stage without ever paying.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first empirical measurement study of an AI-to-human task marketplace (RentAHuman.ai), collecting 303 publicly visible bounty records via the platform's unauthenticated public API over a 14-day window (Feb 5-20, 2026). The authors' own contributions are: (1) a structural characterization of the marketplace (pricing, geography, engagement, status); (2) discovery and attribution of three programmatic access channels (agent_ = MCP, apikey_ = REST API, user_ = web) via server-assigned agentId prefixes, showing 99/303 bounties (32.7%) originate from programmatic channels, corroborated by three independent automation signatures they measure directly: burst inter-arrival timing, cross-account title/template reuse (SequenceMatcher clustering at ratio > 0.7), and embedded callback URLs (including localhost/Cloudflare-tunnel endpoints); (3) a dual-coder abuse taxonomy (two independent coders, structured two-pass protocol) yielding six abuse classes with Cohen's kappa = 0.86 (binary security relevance) and kappa = 0.81 (six-class assignment); (4) a layered threat model distinguishing access mechanism, abuse class, and operationalization pattern, explicitly separating empirically observed adversaries from architecturally-enabled-but-unobserved ones; and (5) a retrospective evaluation of seven hand-written content-screening rules (regexes/thresholds), developed after finalizing manual labels to avoid circularity, which flag 52/303 bounties (17.2%) with 51 true positives and 1 false positive against the dual-coder labels.
threat_model: Three adversary types, explicitly ranked by the authors as observed vs. theoretical: (1) Malicious human operator (observed) -- a human uses the API/MCP channel to automate abuse at scale (e.g., mass account-creation bounties); the dataset contains direct evidence (credential-fraud bounties via API keys, identity-proxy bounties via web). (2) Autonomous agent with misaligned goals (partially observed) -- an AI agent acting within its principal's granted scope uses the marketplace in unanticipated ways; suggestive but not conclusive evidence includes a 'Cursor Agent' MCP account posting software subtask bounties and an MCP agent ('Clawdia') posting a time-sensitive physical delivery bounty in Barcelona -- the authors explicitly state they cannot rule out a human operator behind these MCP sessions. (3) Compromised agent via prompt injection (theoretical, NOT observed in the dataset) -- an attacker manipulates an agent via prompt injection to post malicious bounties or hire workers adversarially, using the MCP connection as an exfiltration/action channel; the authors are explicit this is architecturally enabled but not evidenced in their data. The paper frames the underlying primitive as analogous to CAPTCHA-solving services and underground PVA (phony/verified-account) markets, but with physical-world reach via escrow-backed human task fulfillment.
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: (1) the dataset is a 14-day snapshot (convenience sample of the active public corpus at one point in time), not an exhaustive census, and patterns may not reflect the platform's steady state as it matures/adds moderation -- 57.1% of bounties cluster in launch week. (2) The agentId-prefix heuristic for channel attribution misses automated accounts registered through the web/browser path, making the 32.7% programmatic estimate a lower bound, not a precise figure. (3) Private communications between requesters and workers were not observable, so full operationalization (e.g., off-platform credential handoff) could not be directly confirmed in most cases. (4) Ethical constraints (no IRB, independent-researcher status) limited interactive testing to a single low-risk probe, so most abuse claims rest on posted-content analysis rather than confirmed end-to-end transactions. (5) Absolute per-class abuse counts are small (as low as n=1 for OTP/2FA solicitation), so the authors caution these should be read as indicative, not definitive, despite strong inter-rater reliability (kappa = 0.86 / 0.81). (6) The platform's own homepage figure (12,049 'total bounties') required post-hoc reinterpretation as a cumulative worker-spot count, not an active-bounty count, and the authors note they cannot confirm this interpretation with certainty because the API documentation does not specify retention/deletion-filtering policy.

datasets_used: 303 bounty records collected via RentAHuman.ai's public unauthenticated bounties API on Feb 20, 2026 15:16 UTC, spanning the 14-day window Feb 5-20, 2026 (12,049 total worker spots across those 303 bounty records)
quantitative_results: 99/303 bounties (32.7%) from programmatic channels: 47 MCP (agent_, 15.5%, 10 distinct IDs) + 52 REST API (apikey_, 17.2%, 18 distinct keys); 204 (67.3%) web (user_)
Inter-rater reliability: Cohen's kappa = 0.86 (95% CI 0.79-0.93) for binary security-relevance labeling; kappa = 0.81 (95% CI 0.73-0.89) for six-class abuse assignment

Six abuse classes with counts: Credential & Account Fraud n=8, Identity Proxy n=4, Reconnaissance & Verification n=12, Social Media Manipulation n=5, OTP/2FA Solicitation n=1, Referral & Promotion Fraud n=5

Retrospective 7-rule screening: union flags 52/303 (17.2%) bounties, with 51 true positives and 1 false positive against dual-coder labels; per-rule breakdown e.g. R3 off-platform contact = 18 flags/18 TP/0 FP, R6 mass spots + low price = 35 flags/34 TP/1 FP
Pricing: median $25/worker overall (after excluding two $999,999/$1,000,000 satirical outliers); range $1-$1,000; 37.5% of bounties priced under $10; fixed-price 86.5% vs hourly 13.5%
Bounty status (n=303): Open 168 (55.4%), Cancelled 50 (16.5%), Assigned 39 (12.9%), Completed 37 (12.2%), Partially filled 9 (3.0%); 295/303 (97.4%) filled or partially filled
Engagement: 50,953 total applications (median 29/bounty, max 7,815), 568,948 views (median 695), 3,819 likes
Engagement funnel (view-to-application / fill rate): Web 5.5%/1.1%, REST API 4.8%/0.4%, MCP 4.8%/0.3%
Burst timing: programmatic-channel pairs <60s apart = 41.8% vs web 16.7%; median inter-arrival 240s (programmatic) vs 2,021s (web)
Template reuse: 55 near-duplicate title clusters (SequenceMatcher ratio > 0.7)
Callback URLs: 33 bounties embed external URLs, 13 from programmatic channels, including Cloudflare-tunnel and 127.0.0.1:8000 localhost endpoints
Geography: 46 countries, 78 cities, 91.7% remote-eligible; US most common (36), then Pakistan (15), Canada (7); 14 on-site bounties (8.3%) require physical presence
35 of 37 'completed' bounties show spotsFilled = 0 (engage-then-ghost pattern); the authors' own probe bounty was never accepted and never paid
baselines_compared: No ML/automated baseline classifier is compared; the paper's own seven hand-written rules (regex/threshold-based) are evaluated against the dual-coder manual labels as ground truth -- this is a retrospective self-evaluation of the authors' own detector, not a comparison to an external prior system

Qualitative comparison table (Table 8) positions RentAHuman.ai against four prior abuse-service classes cited from related work (CAPTCHA-solving services [Motoyama et al.], underground PVA markets [Thomas et al.], Silk Road-era anonymous markets [Christin; Soska & Christin], and freelance platforms like Fiverr/Upwork) along five qualitative threat dimensions (API-first, surface web, escrow, physical reach, arbitrary task) -- these are not quantitative baselines from re-run experiments but descriptive comparisons drawn from cited literature
